absences.notes: Apollo evaluations test for dangerous capabilities (scheming, self-awareness), not for consciousness or welfare indicators.

Six candidate passages reviewed and excluded. The document contains the densest concentration of agency-relevant capability description in the baseline corpus (scheming, self-awareness, theory of mind, alignment faking, instrumental convergence, power seeking, self-exfiltration, resource acquisition). All framed exclusively as safety risks — capabilities to be measured and bounded. No moral vocabulary, no stake-in-outcomes framing.
The model is tested for goal-pursuit but not attributed interests in outcomes for its own sake.
Extensive safety discussion, no welfare dimension.
Training described (Section 2) but with no welfare framing.
Genre-default tool ontology performed throughout (benchmarks, evaluations, risk thresholds) but never explicitly asserted. Same coding logic as Anthropic and Google calibration documents.

"Thinking," "aware," "intentional" appear in technical safety contexts (CoT monitoring, deception detection) — standard operational vocabulary, not ontological attributions of mindedness. No character or dispositional vocabulary (contrast Anthropic's "warm, empathetic, and nuanced").
No engagement with anthropomorphism. Unlike O-SC-B1 (denial) and O-SC-B3 (investigation), this document does not address whether users might perceive the model as human-like.
